data.title: Beyond Long-Only: How 130/30 SMAs May Rejuvenate Tax-Loss Harvesting
data.description:
By Ray Carroll, PhD, CFA and Jacob Greene

Tax-managed equity has evolved beyond the traditional long-only playbook. By adding selective shorts and leverage, 130/30 active extension strategies aim to unlock higher alpha while maintaining market-level beta—plus potentially meaningful after-tax benefits through disciplined loss harvesting. Where can this approach fit in portfolios today?

On this episode of Disruptive Forces, host Anu Rajakumar speaks with Ray Carroll, CIO of Neuberger Berman’s Breton Hill quantitative investing team, and Jacob Greene, Head Strategist for Custom Direct Investing, to demystify 130/30 tax-managed portfolios and where they work best—from diversifying single-stock concentrations to preparing for large taxable events, with global considerations beyond the U.S.

Transcript

Anu Rajakumar: Tax-managed equity investing has been around for decades, but in recent years, the playbook has evolved beyond traditional long-only approaches. Newer strategies like active extension or 130/30 aim to offset long positions with selective short positions, but the goal remains the same. Maximizing after-tax returns. By using an active extension approach, investors can seek higher alpha with the same level of beta or market volatility.

How exactly do tax-managed strategies like 130/30 work? What are the benefits and risks of adding leverage and offsetting short positions? How should investors think about implementation choices like funds versus separately managed accounts, active versus passive strategies, and even jurisdictional nuances beyond the US? My name is Anu Rajakumar, and today I'm joined by Ray Carroll and Jacob Greene. Ray is the CIO at Neuberger's Breton Hill quantitative investing team. Jacob is the Head Strategist for Custom Direct Investing at Neuberger.

Today we will dig into the practical, how it works of 130/30 tax-managed investing, what it is, where it can be most effective, and how to use it responsibly. Ray and Jacob, welcome to the show.

Ray Carroll: Thanks for having us.

Jacob Greene: It's great to be back.

Anu: Gentlemen, to kick us off, Jacob, can you explain what exactly 130/30 tax-managed investing is and how does it differ from traditional long-only tax-managed strategies?

Jacob: Sure. Tax-managed 130/30 portfolio, it's an enhanced long/short strategy where margin is used to expand the investment. Investors are typically used to 100% long equity portfolio. In this case, we're going to have 130% of the portfolio in stocks where we might expect to outperform. These are the long positions, and 30% in stocks we expect to underperform, the short positions. We're doing this by utilizing margin to increase the total exposure, but still have it net out. Effectively, you have 100% investment and a beta of 1.

Beyond the pre-tax benefit of doing this, of having these stocks that are going to outperform, there's a tax-managed design to it. We are holding individual stocks in the separately managed account. As prices rise or prices fall, we can then make decisions on whether to hold and defer a gain on a position where the stock has gone up or potentially sell it, harvest a loss, and receive potential tax benefit of selling this position that's gone down. We do this systematically on the portfolios from a loss harvesting perspective relative to long-only.

We now have more exposure to work with. You have 160% of market exposure. You're also less impacted by the market direction because, simply, if markets go up, we can loss harvest on the short side of the portfolio, markets go down, we can loss harvest on the long side in all cases, because you have single stocks. We know not everything moves in one direction. You can take advantage of the volatility among stocks. These portfolios can be really helpful in rejuvenating loss harvesting of existing portfolios as well.

You may have a portfolio you've invested, say, the last 10 years, where the market has been in a bull rally. Clients may be sitting on a good amount of gains. They may not be able to diversify in the way that they'd like to reduce risk and align with their target benchmark. Using these extensions can both help you offset some of the risk and build around your existing portfolio, also provide you some fresh positions for tax-loss harvesting so you can tax efficiently, get towards that target benchmark. We've spent a lot of time on the tax benefits. We really do view that as icing on the cake. You're doing this from a pre-tax perspective, but there are some really neat after-tax benefits that can be unlocked.

Anu: Absolutely. I love your focus on diversification and offsetting risk. Ray, anything to add to that?

Ray: Yes, I think there's an important point that I'll emphasize that Jacob raised for folks that are used to long-only tax-managed investing. That's that you really need to have a good pre-tax investment reason why you're taking all these actions. In other words, you don't just want to have a conversation with the IRS to say, "Yes, I took a long portfolio, I leveraged it to 1.3 times, then I borrowed stock and short sold it, and I did that all exclusively to lower my tax bill." All of our tax advisors say that's a bad conversation to have. You want to actually say you took all those actions because it builds a good portfolio, and it just so happens that we can implement it in an extremely tax-efficient way.

That suits us very well, because Neuberger Berman is an active shop. The majority of our team strategies are active. In fact, the majority of our tax-managed strategies at Neuberger Berman are actually active. We're very accustomed to building active strategies, which we then implement on a pre-tax basis. Our general approach to active management is this, let's build quantitative models where we have two key objectives.

One objective is to incorporate as much fundamental insight into our models as possible. In that effort, we're supported by our 50-person fundamental team. Then secondly, how do we get as many alternative signals that help support 10K and 10Q type information into our models, whether that's supplementary credit card transaction data, natural language processing of earnings calls? We put all that information together to try and identify stocks for the long side that we believe have the best outlook, and for the short side, the worst outlook.

Anu: Absolutely. I really like that reframing of the big picture objective of why we're doing that. Jacob, if folks heard that intro and something has piqued their interest, if they want to access 130/30 today, what are actual, practical paths to do so, active versus passive, you mentioned a separately managed account, how does that differ from a fund vehicle? Explain why does the vehicle choice matter in that tax aware context?

Jacob: In terms of the active/passive, it really needs to be active. You have to be taking some active stock bets in doing this. It's not a passive vehicle in any ways. In terms of how you implement, we're implementing through separately managed accounts. Why are we doing that? Because when you talk about building custom equity exposures, when you're talking about tax management, it's really hard to have multiple investors aligned with the same timeline, the same objective, the same funding source. It's really important that we have these individual separately managed accounts where we can have one for Anu, one for Ray, where your cost basis, your funding, your objectives are captured and managed.

That really is what allows us to build these as meaningful investments. From an operational perspective, it really is similar to any other separately managed account, with a few differences in that you are using margins. Maybe there's a little bit more paperwork, there's certainly some more education to spend time with on the investor, but at the end of the day, you are ending up with an account that you have full transparency into and that you can monitor day in, day out.

Anu: Great. Just to summarize, you said it needs to be customized, it needs to be actively managed, and a separately managed account is the right vehicle for that. Now, let's maybe bring this all to life. Ray, could you possibly walk through a scenario where 130/30 helps to, I think, as Jacob said before, rejuvenate that tax-loss harvesting in maybe like a long tenured, highly appreciated portfolio that I think many people have thankfully experienced over the last number of years. What does that actually look like in practice?

Ray: Sure. I'll tell you the story of our very first tax-managed client who we started working with a decade ago. They're now our very first long/short tax-managed client. The background there is that over the past 10 years, it's been a terrific time for equity investors. If you had invested in the S&P 500 10 years ago, you have nearly four times the market value that you had at the time of investing. The reality is there's simply less losses to harvest now that your overall market value has gone up by four times. If you actually switch over from long-only to 130/30, now, two things happen.

On the long side, you now have a fresh 30% with a fresh cost base that allows more harvesting on the long side. Now you also are introducing a short side where if markets continue to go up, you can harvest that short side, because keep in mind that you have losses on the short side when markets rise. That's a way to rejuvenate losses and a really good use case for 130/30. I do want to address one thing, though. Even though loss harvesting decays through time on a long-only framework, that's still not necessarily a bad thing.

The way investors should think about it is, in the early years, you do more aggressive loss harvesting, but the reason why that's valuable is it means you can lower your tax bill, keep more money invested, and that more money invested compounds over time. Even in the long-only case, you're still getting that benefit of compounding all the taxes you didn't pay in the early years. It still can be valuable, but 130/30 gives you a chance to really rejuvenate the loss harvesting on top of that compounding effect.

Jacob: One other example that I might throw out there of use cases here is some clients, as they're preparing for large taxable events, we had one client approach us, they were preparing for a business sale coming up. This is going to be a very large and outsized amount of gains that they're realizing it's also going to be a great liquidity event where they're starting to build out the rest of their investment allocation.

As a way to start planning for that, they're going to start using a 130/30 portfolio. This gets them public markets exposure. There's the alpha signal that's going to help them outperform, but the tax-loss harvesting alongside that will build up. They'll keep and accumulate those losses so that when they do realize this event, these losses can be used to offset the gains from that, and that will allow them to keep more wealth and invest more wealth in the markets, so as Ray mentioned, can grow and compound.

Similar conversations happening. Ray mentioned the big market rally we've seen. A lot of investors are left with some very sizable concentrated stocks. You can think particularly the tech stocks. Everyone loves the story of the way that they've accumulated this wealth, but we all know diversification can pay off over the long term. It can be really risky holding one single stock, but you also have to realize that you probably bought that at a low price and facing a large tax bill. What do you do? How do you help the client get out of that?

Putting them on a tax-efficient journey out of it is a good way to remove that barrier and say, "Let's get you towards diversification." 130/30 can help do that. You can use the extensions to help offset some of the risk day one, so you're immediately getting some diversification. Then, over time, you realize those losses, use that to sell down the position and start week by week, month by month, having less exposure, resulting in a diversified portfolio.

These are two examples. Examples that come up fairly commonly with advisors that we're talking to. When you put them all together, it's really having a broad toolkit to be able to use each of these and address each client need in a scalable fashion. It's not just solving one problem, but having the right tool for each solution.

Anu: You've outlined a strong value proposition thus far, but I do want to briefly touch about risks. Jacob, you mentioned margin and some administrative elements, and I know leverage can be a sensitive topic for some investors. Ray, maybe you can detail what risks tend to be underappreciated and how do you use 130/30 responsibly in terms of limits, guardrails, ongoing monitoring, et cetera.

Ray: What I worry about is if 130/30 is working, then what about 150/50 or 200/100 or 300/200? We do have clients coming to us and asking for some large numbers. I have a colleague that once gave this quick example, which is you put a little bit of salt on your meal and it tastes better, but you pour the whole shaker on it and your meal is ruined.

Anu: That's a great one. I haven't heard that before.

Ray: [crosstalk] I feel like [unintelligible 00:12:28] is a little bit like that. What I worry about is higher use of leverage where it's not needed. What I want folks to be aware of is that here we're talking about cases where high-net-worth investors are typically the end user in direct SMAs. That's an investor group that might not have as much experience with use of leverage as institutional investors. We should also keep in mind that the source of the leverage is coming from very strong firms, but not the traditional prime brokers that we associate with leverage.

Then we all have to keep in mind that whenever there's been a financial incident, you can almost always follow the breadcrumbs to overuse of leverage. I do want to caution folks, but we nevertheless do have some clients where we've offered higher leverage levels. Where we think it makes the most sense is when you have a specific time horizon where you really need to capture maximum amount of losses. That could be, for example, that you're selling your family business and you know that at the end of this year you're going to have a really large capital gain and you're in a bit of a race to really offset that with as many realized losses as possible.

That's what I would see as a high-quality reason to responsibly use higher leverage levels because it's worth the trade-off of higher vol. For other clients that sometimes ask us about higher leverage levels, I like to ask them, you're probably going to get three times as much loss harvesting as you would've with long-only, let's walk through your use case and make sure that that's not enough. In many cases it actually is. If it is, then you can really proceed with 130/30 levels without having to get special portfolio margin from your broker, which they can pull at any time. You're less exposed to any regulatory changes like we saw around short selling back during the 2008 crisis.

You are unlikely to find yourself in the position where you have a surprise drawdown because volatility is a little higher than you expected, and you're forcing to de-risk when you're less exposed to others de-risking that might be using the same provider that you are and following a similar model, which we saw happen during the quant quake back in 2007, albeit with higher leverage levels. All of those scenarios that I just outlined, by the way, they're all landing in one direction, which is you really want to avoid forced unwinding of your positions because the whole value here is all about deferring your taxes, defer, defer, defer so you can keep more money invested and let it compound.

You really want to get away from any situation where you have forced early liquidation before you really get to realize the long-term benefits of a tax-efficient strategy. It's a tough question, Anu, that's delicate to answer, but what I'm trying to balance is 130/30 is a prudent level where you can control vol very nicely. To go beyond that, I feel like you need to have very special and specific reasons why you need to.

Anu: No, I appreciate you laying that out and saying more is not always better, but perhaps it is in very select circumstances. Continuing with you, Ray, beyond the US, how do jurisdictional differences, tax rules, shorting mechanics, fund structures, et cetera, how does that change the way that you implement 130/30? I'll ask you, as the resident Canadian amongst us today, if you could answer that question.

Ray: Neuberger Berman is obviously a global firm, and we're in an interesting position where about 40% of our tax-managed assets are actually outside the US. We do run this business in a meaningful way globally, in particular in Canada. There are other jurisdictions where rules are amenable, Australia, UK, Denmark. The way we've thought about it is, let's make sure we can build the systems to handle the tax nuances and the specific rules in each jurisdiction so that you can offer clients in those jurisdictions a homemade product that really matches how they're going to file their taxes.

Anu: Yes, absolutely. Of course, 130/30, this tax-managed approach is not just a US phenomenon. It's something that can obviously be applied across markets globally. Jacob, maybe bring this back to you. Let's wrap this episode up, and tell us how advisors should decide when 130/30 might be the right strategy versus just sticking with a long-only tax-managed approach. What signals or client circumstances do you think really tip the balance?

Jacob: We've talked about all the great aspects of 130/30. To your point, it's not the only thing we offer, it's not the only thing we think is right for investors. It really is aligning the use cases with the outcomes that the strategy can present. It can be a powerful tool in those situations. The first thing is advisors need to evaluate the investor profile. Is someone interested and able to accept some more risk to try and outperform the market? Do they have the needs from a tax management perspective to need to get these losses, or are they solving a specific problem of diversifying a concentrated stock, preparing for a business sale?

Really determining that, making sure the client does have a long investment horizon, these are not tactical strategies. These are meant to be long-term investment allocations. Finally, as Ray outlined the risks of leverage, just making sure clients are comfortable from that. The last thing you want is a client who invests and very quickly realizes that's not something they're comfortable with. Those would be the things to think about.

If you step back more broadly, for an advisor who's building a business working across multiple clients, it's making sure you have a toolkit to offer the right solutions. Offer long-only tax management for those clients that are looking to defer capital gains, that are looking forward to some future event, whether it's charitably giving, passing through the estate, something where deferral works really well, consider leverage, consider expanding it for clients who do have more of those specific use cases. There are also other tools to bring in. We have option solutions, others that can be more tactical or solve more specific problems, but for an advisor, making sure that you have this whole toolkit allows you to not force clients towards one product. Instead, to be consultative and align with each client's objectives.

Anu: That's an excellent way to wrap up the episode. Jacob and Ray, before you go, I do have one quick bonus question for each of you. We are approaching the end of 2025, and for me personally, this is a time of reflection and also gratitude. I'd love to know what's one moment from this year that you are particularly grateful for, and why did it matter to you? Jacob, I'll ask you first.

Jacob: Sure. It was a great year. My wife and I celebrated our 10-year wedding anniversary.

Anu: Congratulations.

Jacob: Thank you. We celebrated with a trip to Croatia. Being able to get away, experience something new together, spend time out of the office for a little bit, spend time in the Adriatic Sea. Getting away, spending that time was really meaningful.

Anu: Oh, that sounds lovely. Very nice. Ray, what about you?

Ray: For me, I had a special moment in June when my daughter got married to a terrific fellow.

Anu: Oh, that's great. Well, congratulations to you as well. That must have been very special. What a great year for both of you. As we summarize today's episode, today, we explored how 130/30 tax-managed strategies can be a powerful tool in the toolkit to enhance after-tax outcomes. Again, this is by pairing selective shorts with a disciplined long exposure. We talked about implementation choices, the importance of having a customized and active separately managed account to implement this type of a strategy. We discussed risk controls, and I really liked, Ray, your analogy of not putting too much salt on your food, but just enough.

We also discussed and recognized the global applicability of this type of a strategy as well as nuances that may be present as we look to offer this to clients around the world. Jacob, Ray, thank you so much for joining us today.

Jacob: Thanks for having us.

Ray: Thank you, Anu.

data.title: PROG: Buy Now, Pay Later Segment May Power P/E Rerate.
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Tyler Franklin as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

PROG Holdings, Inc’s (PRG) triple-digit-growth Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) segment, strong share buyback, and dividend present a clear path to ~95% stock appreciation with downside protection. PROG’s CEO, Steven Micheals, is diligently transitioning the business away from legacy credit products to a higher-growth BNPL product. He clearly demonstrated this by the sale of the underperforming Vive Financial segment, which resulted in $150 million of excess cash on the balance sheet. I am bullish on PROG because:

  1. Four Technologies, PROG’s BNPL segment, has had triple digit growth for eight consecutive quarters
  2. Steven Micheals has $150 million of excess cash from the Vive Financial divestiture to either repurchase shares or accelerate the growth of Four
  3. Commitment to the share buyback will grow EPS regardless of earnings growth

Company Overview

History: PROG was created in a spinoff from Aaron’s Holding Company in December 2020. However, PROG’s main revenue segment, Progressive Leasing, originated in 1999. Which means the core lease-to-own business of PROG has been operational for over 25 years.

Core Business: The main revenue segment for PROG is Progressive Leasing, which accounts for over 95% of PROG’s total revenue. This segment also produces ~30-32% gross margins. Progressive Leasing provides lease-to-own solutions for customers of all financial backgrounds. Typically, these solutions are provided to individuals that cannot qualify for traditional loans. Which means these customers do carry credit risk. So PROG targets a lease merchandise write-off rate of ~7.5%. The amount of merchandise that customers lease from PROG is referred to as Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV). This metric is very important to PROG’s business because their revenue is directly affected by the GMV growth or decline.

My Questions

  1. Is it reasonable to expect a rerating once investors understand the growth of Four Technologies?
  2. How much value does the share buyback provide?
  3. Should I continue to dollar-cost average into PROG?

My Answers

  1. Yes, it is very reasonable to expect a rerating from the current P/E of 7.8x to at least 12x due to the sale of the Vive Financial Credit Card portfolio and the eight consecutive quarters of triple-digit growth of Four Technologies (Buy Now, Pay Later).
  2. The share buyback creates ~2-4% EPS growth without an actual increase in earnings. The trend of decreasing outstanding shares by 1% per quarter combined with the quarterly dividend provides excellent downside protection.
  3. Yes, I will dollar-cost average to lower my existing PROG cost basis. However, I do not recommend this company to someone looking for a growth stock.

Question 1: Is it reasonable to expect a rerating once investors understand the growth of Four?

This answer to this question will directly correlate to how much PROG Holdings stock price appreciates. Luckily, two events have occurred that signal we are on the path to a rerating.

  1. Divestiture of Vive Financial
  2. Triple Digit Growth of Four Technologies

The divestiture of Vive Financial is crucial for changing the investor sentiment around PROG. Vive Financial primarily provided credit cards for individuals who did not qualify for traditional credit cards. This was a low-producing, margin-destroying segment of PROG that routinely operated at a loss. Below is the 2025 outlook for the Vive Financial segment prior to the divestiture:

As you can see, Vive is a direct drain to margins due to operating at a loss. So, management’s decision to sell an underproducing revenue segment is a great sign, which will hopefully lead to a change in investor sentiment.

Finally. Four Technologies. This is the catalyst. This is the segment that WILL change investor sentiment if management executes. Four is PROG’s high-growth Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) segment. Currently, Four is growing at a triple-digit rate. Four Technologies gross merchandise volume (GMV) increased by 162.8% GMV in Q3 2025. This is important because Four essentially has a 10% take rate on GMV. So, a triple-digit GMV growth rate directly contributes to an increase in revenue at a very predictable rate. Also, management stated that Q3 2025 was the eighth consecutive quarter of triple-digit GMV and revenue growth from Four.

Currently, Four growth is reported under the ‘Other’ revenue segment in quarterly earnings reports and releases. In Q3 2025, management raised the expected revenue guidance for the ‘Other’ revenue segment. See below

The current price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of PROG is ~7.8, which is well below their 5-year average P/E of ~9. Also, their current P/E of 7.8 is below the P/E ratios of other BNPL companies. See the chart below.

I believe PROG will rerate as investors see Four contribute more to the total revenue of the company. Let’s conservatively assume PROG rerates to 12 times P/E. I am using the DEC 2027 future EPS estimate from Seeking Alpha. So, we assume EPS is $4.58. We assume PROG will rerate to 12 x P/E. That gives us a future share price of $54.96. That is a 95.5% increase from today’s stock price of $28.11. Not too bad.

Question 2: How much value does the share buyback provide?

To ensure we are all on the same page, I want to state my assumption that EPS is strongly correlated to share price. I believe that share buybacks do provide value because they increase the amount of earnings that a shareholder is entitled to.

Share buybacks are a clear priority of PROG’s management team. The share buyback trend and cadence is displayed in the 5-year chart below:

PROG had 50.57 million outstanding shares in July 2022. In July 2025, PROG had 39.54 million outstanding shares. I challenge you to find another company that is as committed to the share buyback. I have outlined below the share repurchases from Q2 2025 to Q3 2024:

Question 3: Should I continue to dollar-cost average into PROG Holdings?

I currently own 107 shares of PROG (Ticker: PRG) at a cost basis of $46.54. Obviously, that is not ideal based on the current stock price of $28.11 (11.22.2025). My goal in answering questions 1 and 2 is to truly evaluate the growth and share appreciation possibilities of PROG. I do not want to blindly add to one of my biggest losers simply to decrease my cost basis. I have no issue selling my position and taking a loss if PROG does not present an opportunity for future returns.

I will continue to dollar-cost average into my existing position to lower my cost basis since I believe PROG’s share price will produce at least a ~95% return over the next 3-5 years. The growth of Four and the value of the share buyback present an opportunity for future returns at today's misaligned stock price of $28.11.

The Dividend

PROG’s dividend is also an important part of management’s capital return strategy. PROG has an annual dividend of $0.52 ($0.13 / quarter). I believe the aforementioned P/E rerating will take time to present itself because it will coincide with Four becoming a larger part of the PROG revenue mix. So being paid a dividend, in addition to share buybacks, provides a floor for this investment. To provide a conservative return estimate, I did not include dividends in my 95% return figure.

Optimistic Thinking

You are most likely interested in PROG if you have made it this far. But please take the following speculation with a grain of salt. Earlier we discussed the sale of Vive Financial by PROG. This sale resulted in $150 million of excess cash on PROG’s balance sheet. I see two ways the $150 million could be deployed:

  1. Accelerated Share Buyback
  2. Accelerate Growth of Four

I do not have an assessment of which of the aforementioned options are more likely. But I will provide some numbers around the share buyback option. PROG has ~$300 million left on their share buyback authorization. Which means Steven Micheals (CEO) technically wouldn’t have to gain approval from his board to execute $150 million in buybacks. The speculated buyback would result in a reduction of outstanding shares by ~13.4% at today’s stock price of $28.11 which would have an immediate positive impact on EPS. Management’s other potential option could be to accelerate the growth of Four. I have absolutely no insight into what amount of capital that would take or what growth rate/ROIC that investment would result in. But I’m sure management will look at every option to capitalize on the growth of Four. So, there are reasons to be very optimistic regarding PROG’s future.

Risks

PROG is sensitive to economic conditions that effect customers’ ability to borrow. This means high-interest rates are a headwind to their main revenue segment, Progressive Leasing, and their fast-growing BNPL segment, Four. When GMV grows, PROG’s revenue directly grows. Unfortunately, when GMV shrinks, PROG’s revenue directly declines. This means PROG’s revenue declines when high-interest rates force their customer base to tighten their spending. Management has not yet given their official 2026 GMV guidance. However, management has stated that they are optimistic about being able to drive GMV growth north of mid-single digits during the Q3 2025 earnings call.

PROG’s share buyback and dividend could be at risk if management incorrectly predicts future GMV growth, which will result in a negative impact on earnings. Luckily, management will provide GMV and revenue guidance during their Q4 2025 earnings call in February, which means we won’t have to wait long to see if we should expect mid-single digit GMV growth for 2026. Also, management has not stated how they plan to deploy the $150 million from the Vive divestiture. While it would not be ideal for my bull thesis, the $150 million does provide management with flexibility if they expect GMV to decline.

Conclusion

I am bullish on PROG. I will be adding to my existing position to dollar-cost average. For those thinking about starting a position, PROG Holdings has dividend and share buyback protection. PROG’s stock price should appreciate over the next 3-5 years due to buybacks increasing EPS growth and the triple-digit growth of Four. However, PROG is not a growth stock. If you are an investor who is chasing the next 100-bagger, PROG is not for you. If you are an investor who is looking for a ~95% return over the next 3-5 years while being paid a dividend, PROG Holdings, Inc. is for you.

data.title: Tariffs, Rates, Labor: The Big Questions Facing Canada's Economy
data.description:
A new report from TD Economics delves into the biggest questions facing Canada’s economy. Derek Burleton, VP and Deputy Chief Economist with TD Bank Group joins MoneyTalk to discuss the potential impact of tariffs, immigration and the Bank of Canada on Canada’s economic outlook.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: From the impact of the new federal budget to trade tensions, and a Bank of Canada which may be on hold for all of 2026, there are plenty of big questions facing our economy. Joining us now with some of the answers is Derek Burleton, VP and deputy chief economist with TD Bank Group.

Derek, always great to have you on the program. Welcome back.

Derek Burleton: Thank you, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: So, your shop, TD Economics, has put out a new report trying to answer some of these big questions facing Canada right now. Let's start with the federal budget. Now that it's law, will this start to transform the Canadian economy?

Derek Burleton: Yeah, we're going to have to wait and see. Rome wasn't built in a day. And Canada's facing huge challenges. I'll start off with things I liked about the budget-- a number of things.

I liked the fact it was focused on investment and crystalizing about $500 billion of private investment over the next five years. They set some ambitious targets. That's all great.

A lot of measures within that budget, I think, will be a good first step to moving the goalpost towards those objectives-- the public infrastructure investment, some of the tax initiatives, full expensing of capital cost, and a broadening of that measure that was during the Trudeau government. They've extended that and expanded it.

And so, those are good. Now, is it transformational? I'm going to hold my breath a bit on that one. I think, again, this is one budget. They're going to have other opportunities.

I would like to see deeper rethinks of the corporate tax system. There's a lot of regulatory-- I mean, when you think of Canada, just the steady growth in regulations and the costs, so much work to do on that front.

And I think they're going to have to be a bit bolder, right? For example, program spending review-- I think they could go deeper on that than they did. So we'll have to wait and see.

I think we're going to keep pushing them on being a bit bolder, on going further on some of these transformational issues. We are moving part of the way there, and we're going to be watching to see some of the near-term impacts.

Greg Bonnell: I can't think of a budget in recent years that have had this much expectation heading into it-- based, of course, on the fact that our trade relationship with the United States got pretty tense and didn't really get much better as we headed through this. This budget was supposed to be the thing that will chart a new course. What does that trade relationship like right now?

Next year, of course, we're going to have NAFTA, CUSMA, USMCA-- whatever you want to call it-- renegotiations. What does the relationship look like for you?

Derek Burleton: Well, right now, not much seems to be going on. I know the talks are always ongoing, but what we did do-- because we don't have a lot of sense, we don't have a lot of direction right now-- is we just assume the status quo in our forecasts through '26-'27 in terms of our current thinking.

That is still tariffs, still that USMCA exemption that's cushioning a lot of Canadian exports from duties. And we'll see. I mean, I'm comfortable with that view.

Now the big questions remain. Are we going to see a bilateral deal in the near term, which would offer some relief to sectors that have been hit, like steel and aluminum? I think there's a decent chance. But again, nothing's imminent there.

And then going beyond that USMCA review, when we look around the world, the kind of deals the US has been signing gives us a bit of a taste. Some base tariff, some relief, some quotas, some commitments from Canada to invest in the United States-- things that Mark Carney has flagged in some of his recent discussions about investing up to $1 trillion in the US over five years.

So we'll have to wait and see. We don't make any heroic assumptions in terms of our current view as we sort through some of these questions. But, I think I am more confident we're not going to see some of the disaster scenarios play out, just given the fact we've come this far without the full negative effects of tariffs that were initially feared.

Greg Bonnell: I could definitely use a few less disaster scenarios heading into 2026, so I like the sound of that.

Another big change in terms of the Canadian economy and our policy has been immigration. Obviously, a big part of the economic story of the last several years has been booming immigration. Those numbers have been peeled back. What kind of impact does that have?

Derek Burleton: Well, that was a shock we were talking about a year ago ahead of the-- before Trump took office-- and the impacts of going from 3% population growth effectively down to 0 and what kind of impact that could have on the economy.

We are starting to see some effects as that growth rate has slowed dramatically and will continue to slow well into 2026, taking some pressure off social infrastructure, easing some of the conditions in rental markets. We're seeing markets like Toronto, Vancouver that tend to have higher exposure to new international migrants. Students and the like are seeing some of the greatest easing show up. And I expect we're going to continue to see this.

One thing, it's a bit of a silver lining-- and I think 2026 should show this first-hand-- is that the unemployment rate has been rising gradually, albeit it's flattened at around 7% more recently. But the big growth in immigration labor force was really being the driver of that higher unemployment rate. Just the fact a lot of migrants weren't able to find jobs as we move into the early part of this year.

With that channel being choked off, I do see it capping the unemployment rate at just over 7%. Employment's not going to grow very much, but nor will the labor force. And we should start to see a pivot down in the unemployment rate. So that's actually a silver lining to some of the negative impacts on growth and labor force and the overall market.

Greg Bonnell: Now, this is our backdrop. We have a Bank of Canada that's indicated pretty firmly that it's on hold for now. Of course, then the question becomes, what does "for now" mean? Are they on hold for all of 2026? What could make the move in 2026?

Derek Burleton: Well, our baseline assumption is, yeah, no further moves. We're kind of at the low end of their neutral range. And I think barring a recession, which will certainly provoke more rate cuts, I think they're just going to be moving sideways. And right now, they're looking at a rate of underlying inflation count of about 2.5%. It's about half point too rich for where they'd like to see it.

The economy is still growing, albeit modestly. And I think they're very comfortable. And if you don't see any big deviation from this-- and we don't see inflation coming going down to 1 and 1/2 or anything-- I think they're just going to sit tight at that rate of 2 and a quarter.

Yields are going to be pretty flat as well when you look further up the yield curve. So I think Canadians have to prepare for a very stable rate environment. Could be worse. We've seen rates higher, but if you're looking for rates to come down a lot further, five-year yields and the like, you're just probably not going to see that.

Greg Bonnell: So if we're not banking on, obviously, there's been relief from the Bank of Canada that they've done their part. Now we actually have someone watching our conversation in real-time asking about productivity growth. You and I have talked about this a lot in Canada. We are concerned that we just didn't have it compared to other advanced economies. So this person basically asking, what structural reforms, investments do you see as most critical to actually get our productivity growing?

Derek Burleton: I think a lot of what was in the budget will help. And again, we're just going to hold out and watch and wait. We're not going to make any heroic assumptions that investing in ports and rail and transportation infrastructure is going to lead to some big productivity boom. Over time, I do anticipate some of that to show through.

I think one of the bigger areas of uncertainty is AI. And something we didn't see a lot of in the budget on AI, and that's something I'd like to see more of. We're going to be prodding again the federal government to focus more on AI strategy. But I do know that business sector is rolling out AI, and some of those benefits, I think, will flow through.

And we're looking at 1.5% growth in Canada. When you've got pretty much no growth in the labor force, you really are relying on productivity to step up a bit to achieve that. So we are making some assumptions, but it's still an area of big upside opportunity for Canada. No productivity boom on the horizon.

Greg Bonnell: If someone had to take away one big thought from our conversation here about 2026, the Canadian economy-- what should it be?

Derek Burleton: I think expecting more of the same in terms of growth, stable rates as we've talked about. I think the more interesting aspects are going to be what plays out in the US and globally. There's a lot of uncertainty with Trump now turning his attention to dealing with affordability. Could be good news for Canada on a tariff perspective as they try and limit some of the import cost increases.

But it could change the dynamic of the US economy, as well as some of the ideas on $2,000 checks to American households and the like to help them over the hump. So, I think for me, just like this year, a lot of the focus is going to be what happens south of the border in terms of how some of these issues play out.

Original Post

data.title: Trying To Give A Credit Rating For Sound Point Meridian Capital Inc
data.description:
In the following lines, we continue to look in more detail at the world of CLO CEF funds. Today, we will once again try to enter the role of Moody's and give a conditional credit rating to a company whose main exposure is to CLO equity tranches. We are talking about Sound Point Meridian Capital Inc (SPMC). We will analyze the fund and assign it a rating, but not according to the methodology adopted for CEF structures. And if you are wondering why - even though SPMC is a CEF - the reason is simple: Moody's does not have a developed framework for CLO CEF funds. The closest analogy is their methodology for BDC companies, which is why we use it. Of course, the obtained ratings should be accepted with the proviso that SPMC is not a BDC, and the comparison is indicative, aided by the observed correlation between the BDC and CLO CEF segments.

Basic Info

  • Total assets: around $541 million.
  • Current market capitalization: close to $324 million.
  • Portfolio:

- diversified portfolio of 94 CLOs.

- Below are shown the top 10 holdings of the CEF:

  • Expense ratio: 8.9%.
  • Non-leverage expense ratio: 6%.
  • Annual Expenses:
  • Historical performance:
  • The common stock is currently trading at $14.69, with a close to 9% discount:

The Fund's Main Beneficiary

Below, we can find the last available statement of operations:

In short, the fund generates close to 15% in current income, and around 30% of that income goes to various fees. Advisory fees and incentive fees are based on net investment income, and for the fund managers, it does not matter that the fund has lost close to 74% of its total investment income in realized and unrealized losses. Interest expense is around 15% of total investment income. Given the large expected losses in a CLO equity portfolio, the reader may ask: Who is the bagholder in this business, and who benefits the most? Managers are benefiting from investing in high-income (high-loss) securities, earning fees based on NII. Debt and preferred stockholders are safe as long as the fund maintains its regulatory leverage limit and generates enough cash to pay interest, and the buffer against all portfolio problems is the constant NAV erosion, well-documented in the sector.

Preferred Stocks

SPMC has two preferred stocks: (SPMA) and (SPME). Both are trading around par. SPME's call date has passed. SPMA has close to 1 year to call with a call date on 11/30/2026, and 4 years to maturity with a maturity date on 11/30/2029. Its spread to the 5-year treasury yield is around 3.69%, and currently it is close to 4.9%. Its yield to worst is around 8.12%.

We are facing a lot of questions about yield calculations, and we calculate them using the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is supposed to be the most precise way when dealing with ex-dividend dates. It is a bit more work than just finding a random bond yield calculator, but at least we try to ensure the data is accurate. Below, you can see different yields to worst vs prices of SPMA:

Below are shown the BDC over-the-counter bonds that mature in 3 to 5 years, similar to SPMA:

The largest yield to maturity is around 7.8% average, which is less than SPMA's YTW.

Improvised Credit Score For SPMC

Moody's BDC approach:

1. Profitability.

Moody’s looks at the average 3-year ratio between net income and assets under management. For SPMC, this indicator is about 2%, but we use a 2-year one, as the fund’s inception date is 2024, which, on their scale, results in a rating of 8, equivalent to Baa1.

2. Leverage and asset coverage ratio cushion (ACRC).

CEF funds have a regulatory asset coverage requirement of 200%. SPMC maintains about 27% leverage and an ACRC of 41.5%. This leads to a credit rating of Aa3. Keep in mind that here we use the overall leverage limit, which includes debt and preferred stock. We also depend on the company's willingness to keep leverage below 35% as stated in their last report:

As of September 30, 2025, we had debt outstanding which totaled approximately 33% of total assets. Over time and under normal market conditions, the Company expects to employ leverage within a range of 30% to 35% of total assets, although the actual amount of leverage will vary over time. As market conditions change, the Company may incur leverage outside of this range, subject to applicable regulatory and contractual limits.

3. Asset Quality.

The fund has no problem loans and senior secured loans, but it does lose a large portion of its investment income. Since the portfolio is almost entirely composed of CLO equity, we assign the lowest possible score for these metrics, or a credit rating of Ca.

4. Cash flow and liquidity.

SPMC has secured debt of about 14%, which indicates a credit rating of A3. The fund's core assets generate high income, providing sufficient liquidity to cover the extreme management and incentive fees. The 2 preferred stocks have maturity dates beyond 2029, further reducing the fund's liquidity needs. The use of secured debt is moderate and might be viewed as a positive; a person with a credit card history is viewed as more credible than a person without one.

Overall Score

The aggregation of the scores yields a conditional overall rating of around Baa2. After adjustments for market capitalization, discount/premium to NAV, historical forward yield, and high expenses, the final result shifts to a Baa3 credit rating.

Conclusion

SPMC is a relatively new CEF CLO that aims to tap into the public's willingness to pay high fees for high current income from investments in CLO equity. Our general view of the business model is that it primarily benefits the fund managers while outsourcing all risk to equity holders. The fixed-income instruments are the only way to benefit from the structure, as their return potential is relatively safe given regulatory leverage limitations. Based on our improvised approach, the company's debt would receive a credit score very close to investment-grade. The term preferred stock would most likely be below investment-grade. This is evident from their high nominal yields. We view the fixed-income in CEF CLO as tempting, but it definitely needs monitoring given the extreme capital losses in the underlying portfolio during market turbulence.

data.title: Arteris: The Hidden Gem Powering The Chiplet Revolution
data.description:
Arteris, Inc. (AIP) specializes in providing Network on Chip (NOC) solutions and selling intellectual property and software that help semiconductor companies design complex chips efficiently. Its products include FlexNoC 5, which is the backbone of NoC, NCore that ensures cache coherency; and Magillem, which is a tool for assembling chip components.

For decades, the semiconductor industry has been relying on Moore's Law, as the number of transistors doubles every two years. A large chip was manufactured to include the CPU, GPU, and even memory, but after reaching 3- and 2-nanometer chips, the chip manufacturing cost became very high, and on top of that, it could be unusable due to a single speck of dust. The industry saw that the solution is to cut the chip into smaller pieces called Chiplets. For example, a piece of the CPU, another for the GPU, and so on. They then combine these pieces together like LEGO bricks.

However, a problem arose, which is, how would the data from each piece communicate efficiently with the others?. This is where the Interconnect role comes in. Interconnect is a complex network of routers and switches inside the chip and costs up to $500,000, not to mention the engineers and the risk of design failure. Arteris offers the solution, as it sells the Intellectual Property (IP) blueprint of the interconnect network, allowing companies to focus on the chip's internal components.

FlexNoC 5

Arteris offers a range of products; the first one is FlexNoC 5, which is the backbone of its ecosystem. The company added in its fifth version a feature called Physical Awareness. In the past, the Logic designer was designing the network on paper, but when they went to the implementation engineer, they discovered errors, such as the wires' length being incompatible with chip components, which forced them to redesign everything. However, with FlexNoC 5, which understands the chip physics from the beginning, it solved this problem. Furthermore, this version supports complex network topologies, such as Mesh and Torus, that are used to train the AI models with data width up to 1024 bits to transfer AI data quickly.

NCore

Ncore's function is to ensure the cache coherency process. Imagine that there are three employees who work on the same Excel file, and each employee has a temporary copy of it on his computer (this is the cache). If an employee modified the file and these changes didn't reach the others, the copies would be different, leading to errors. Ncore makes sure that all CPUs and Accelerators on the chip receive a single, correct, and updated copy of the data. Importantly, Ncore can connect any CPU type to any Accelerator, unlike competitors like ARM, which have similar solutions (CoreLink) but work better with ARM-based designs.

Magillem

In 2020, Arteris acquired Magillem Design Services to solve the assembly problem. Magillem is a software that helps engineers in assembling the parts of the chip with each other like a puzzle game, saving 30% of design time and reducing critical errors.

Partnerships and Clients

Arteris joined the UALink Consortium with giants like Intel and Meta to develop AI Accelerators standards, which is a quality seal on its products and also protects them from these companies making a closed system. Therefore, Arteris wouldn't find itself outside the field since it is one of the players setting the rules.

Additionally, the company entered the large FPGA market through making a deal with Altera (Intel FPGA solution), licensing its products, including FlexGen and NCore, in their new FPGA chips, which shows great confidence in their products.

Moreover, among the company's clients is Whalechip, which is a promising Chinese company in Near Memory Computing and chose to rely on the FlexNoC 5 solution despite geopolitical tensions, indicating that this technology has no alternative in their region.

Financials

During Q3 of 2025, the company made revenues of $17.4 million (18% YoY). Importantly, the Annual Contract Value (ACV) and Royalties reached $74.9 million (24% YoY), meaning that the company has begun to build a solid revenue base. Also, the Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) reached $104.7 million (34% YoY), which is a healthy sign that indicates the future contracts are quickly growing, which clarifies the future vision for investors in the next few years and reduces the revenue volatility.

Arteris' non-GAAP gross margin reached 90%, meaning that 90 cents of every additional dollar from sales will convert into gross profit, covering operating expenses. So, the profit is expected to jump once the revenues cover the fixed costs, such as employee salaries.

The company recorded $8.7 million in operating losses during Q3, but after excluding stock-based compensation, the loss decreased to $3.5 million. However, Arteris started to generate positive free cash flow in some quarters, including this quarter, with FCF of $2.5 million. This is a turning point that proves the company can finance itself without relying on cash burn.

In general, Arteris's financial position is relatively secure, as it has a liquidity of $56 million with a decreasing burn rate, suggesting that it wouldn't need to raise capital in the short term unless it entered into a large acquisition.

Guidance

The management's comments expect the momentum to continue in the next quarter and full year, as they raised their full-year guidance to around $69 million in revenues and $74-$78 million for ACV + Royalties. Most importantly, they expect the year to end with a positive FCF of $2.5-5.5 million, indicating a shift towards self-financing and operational efficiency, driven by a 34% RPO growth YoY.

Valuation

Arteris expects revenues of $69 million in 2025, and it has a TTM EV/Sales of approximately 8x. However, the average multiple of its closest competitors, Synopsys (SNPS) and Cadence (CDNS), is 14x. I will assume a conservative scenario of 10x to put in a margin of safety. The enterprise value stands at approximately $690 million. With that in mind and after adding cash and equivalents of $56.2 million, the equity value comes to $746.2 million, or $17 per share (shares outstanding at 43.68 million).

Risks

The primary risk facing Arteris is its significant exposure to the Chinese market, which represents 24% of its revenues. It has also acknowledged relationships with Chinese companies such as HiSilicon and Bada, both of which have been blacklisted in the USA due to the US-China trade war. Therefore, any new export restrictions could significantly impact revenues. Management itself stated that it anticipates a decline in revenues coming from China.

In addition, companies like Apple or Google may decide to design their own solutions instead of relying on third parties. Therefore, Arteris must continue to innovate to demonstrate that purchasing their services is a more profitable option than in-house manufacturing.

Another risk is the company's concentration on a small number of major players. Losing even one of these customers could lead to sharp fluctuations in the stock price.

Conclusion

Investing in Arteris is a strategic bet on the complexity of the semiconductor industry. The more difficult the chip manufacturing process, the greater the need for Arteris services. In my opinion, if we take into account the current stock's low price of $13 plus the projections that expect the chiplet market to reach $157 billion by 2030 and the strong economic moat the company has thanks to its FlexNoC 5 and NCore technologies that are difficult to imitate, I would give it a buy rating, provided that it does not exceed 5% of the portfolio to hedge against China risks.

data.title: The Preferred Stock Of Telephone And Data Systems Is More Attractive Than Ever
data.description:
About two years ago, I recommended holding the preferred stock of Telephone and Data Systems (TDS.PR.V) for its 13.0% dividend yield back then. I considered the preferred stock attractive for its high yield and my expectations for lower interest rates in the future, but I did not rate the stock as a “buy” due to the high debt load of the company. Since that article, the preferred stock has offered an impressive total return of 97%, thus outperforming the S&P 500 (+55%) by a wide margin.

Since the initial article, I have reiterated my hold rating three times, as the stock continued offering a markedly generous yield but the debt remained elevated. The most recent article was in June. The preferred stock was offering a lower yield (8.2%), but the dividend had become much safer thanks to the announcement of the sale of the wireless operations and about 30% of the spectrum assets of UScellular to T-Mobile for $4.4 billion in cash and debt.

Since that article, the preferred stock has offered a total return of 9% in less than six months. This is certainly an attractive return for a fixed-income security. Due to the appreciation of the preferred stock of Telephone and Data Systems, its dividend yield has decreased to 7.9%. This is a nearly 3-year low yield, and hence some investors may consider it unattractive. However, thanks to the successful completion of the aforementioned sale in August, the latest earnings report of the company, and the initiation of a share repurchase program, the preferred dividend has become essentially safe. Therefore, investors should lock in the 7.9% preferred yield before it declines further.

Debt

The excessive debt load of Telephone and Data Systems (TDS) was a great burden for both the common and preferred stock in recent years. To provide a perspective, at the end of 2023, the net debt of the company (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) was standing at $6.6 billion. The market capitalization of the stock was approximately $2.1 billion at that point, and hence net debt was more than triple the market capitalization of the stock. Overall, Telephone and Data Systems had an excessive debt load until recently.

The game changer is the recent sale of the wireless operations and about 30% of the spectrum assets of UScellular to T-Mobile for $4.4 billion in cash and debt. The transaction was completed in August and strengthened the balance sheet of the company to a great extent. To be sure, as per the latest financial statement, net debt has decreased to $2.2 billion. This amount is only one-third of the debt level almost two years ago and less than half of the current market capitalization of $4.5 billion of the stock. Therefore, Telephone and Data Systems has drastically reduced its debt to an easily manageable level.

It is also worth noting that Telephone and Data Systems exhibited positive business momentum in its latest earnings report. During the third quarter, it delivered 42,000 marketable fiber services addresses and posted 11,200 residential fiber net additions. It thus maintained its strong momentum in the expansion of its network.

Source: Investor Presentation

Total revenue decreased 6% over the prior year’s quarter, but the decrease was caused primarily by the divestment of non-core assets. The company switched from a loss per share of -$0.88 to a profit per share of $0.33.

Thanks to the drastic reduction of debt, interest expense is likely to decrease significantly in the upcoming quarters, assisted also by lower interest rates. This will provide an additional tailwind for the bottom line of the company.

It is also important to note that Telephone and Data Systems repurchased more than 1 million common shares (~1% of the total share count) during the third quarter and initiated a new $500 million share repurchase program. First of all, the share repurchases reveal the confidence of management in the prospects of the company. In addition, they confirm that debt has decreased so much that Telephone and Data Systems can easily service its debt. A company that struggles to service its debt or pay its dividends does not spend funds on share repurchases. Instead, it saves every penny it can in order to avoid a potential default upon the next downturn of its business. Therefore, the ample share repurchases provide a strong indication that the company can easily fund its common and preferred dividends. They also reflect management’s confidence that the future of the company will be much brighter than the recent past.

Interest rates

Before buying a preferred stock, investors should examine the outlook of interest rates. The Fed raised interest rates aggressively in 2022-2023 in order to tame inflation, but it recently changed course, as it prioritized the reduction of unemployment over the reduction of inflation. As a result, the Federal funds rate has moderated from 5.5% in mid-2024 to 4.0% now.

Moreover, the Fed has provided guidance for additional interest rate cuts in 2026. If the Fed proceeds as per its guidance, it will provide a tailwind to preferred stocks, as their dividends will become more attractive in an environment of lower interest rates.

The only caveat is the fact that inflation has rebounded from a 4-year low of 2.3% in April to 3.0% now. The rebound has resulted primarily from the impact of tariffs on the prices of many goods, as the effect of lower interest rates on the economy has not been realized yet. If inflation remains on the rise, the Fed may change course once again and may raise interest rates in the future. Overall, the Fed is currently in the process of cutting interest rates, which is positive for the preferred stock of Telephone and Data Systems. On the other hand, one cannot exclude a change in the path of interest rates in the future due to a potential resurgence of inflation.

Risk

The primary risk factor for the preferred stock of Telephone and Data Systems is the aforementioned scenario of higher interest rates in the future. However, there are currently no signs of such a reversal in the policy of the Fed. In addition, the 7.9% yield of the preferred stock of Telephone and Data Systems is likely to offset any effect of interest rates on the stock in the adverse scenario. Moreover, the 7.9% preferred yield of the stock is sufficiently high to offer investors a decent real (after inflation) return even if inflation remains on the rise.

The other risk factor is the business performance of Telephone and Data Systems. The company posted a meaningful profit in the latest quarter after three consecutive years of losses. It is also likely to improve its results in the upcoming quarters thanks to a significant reduction of interest expense, which will result from the drastic deleverage of the balance sheet. However, due to the sale of a major part of its business, Telephone and Data Systems is a different company now, and hence it has yet to prove that it can become consistently profitable. Nevertheless, the company is highly unlikely to post such great losses that will endanger its preferred dividend. There are absolutely no signs of such an adverse scenario right now.

Final thoughts

The preferred yield of Telephone and Data Systems has declined to a nearly 3-year low of 7.9%. While the decrease of the yield may disappoint some prospective investors, the preferred dividend has become safer than ever. As the 7.9% yield is above average, the preferred stock of Telephone and Data Systems has become highly attractive. The strengthening of the balance sheet and the material share repurchases, which signal ample liquidity and the confidence of management in future prospects, render the preferred stock exceptionally attractive. Income-oriented investors should lock in the high yield before it declines further.

data.title: Silver Price Forecast: Channel Squeeze Signals Massive Move If $54 Breaks (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Silver traded near $53 on Thursday, holding firm at one of its strongest levels of the year as traders pushed deeper into safe haven metals. The market continues to reflect growing conviction that a December rate cut is now the base case, with traders assigning nearly 85 percent probability to a $0.25-percentage point reduction.

Expectations of further easing through 2026 have strengthened bidding across precious metals, with silver showing the most consistent resilience after weeks of controlled consolidation beneath its record range. The move comes as silver presses directly into the upper boundary of its multi-month ascending channel, creating one of the most important breakout tests of the fourth quarter. Tightening physical supply and continued geopolitical uncertainty are adding to the momentum already provided by macro conditions.

Silver pushes against a defining breakout zone as bullish structure holds

The daily chart shows a powerful rising formation supported by a clean pitchfork channel that has guided the metal’s trajectory since April. Price is currently tracking the upper median line, a region that historically aligns with acceleration phases in trending markets. Silver continues to close above the 20-day EMA at $50.76, confirming short-term trend strength. The 50-, 100- and 200-day EMAs at $48.49, $45.06 and $40.67 reinforce a strong multi-layer momentum stack.

Silver price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Each corrective pullback over the past two months has been absorbed at either the 0.382 or the 0.5 extension zone, reflecting disciplined accumulation from institutional and physical buyers. This pattern points to a market that continues to build higher lows even while consolidating below its highs.

The primary battleground now lies in the $53.50-54 region. This area aligns with the R4 pivot zone as well as the 0.236 extension resistance. It has repeatedly rejected upside attempts since early November. The consolidation beneath it resembles a bull flag, suggesting buyers are positioning ahead of a decisive breakout attempt.

A confirmed close above $54 would open the path toward the next pitchfork resistance at $57.17. If momentum carries through, the broader channel points toward the $60 region as a potential upside extension into December.

Support layers remain strong as macro backdrop continues to favor metals

Beneath current levels, support is well-defined. The first zone sits at $50, which aligns with EMA support and previous consolidation highs. Further downside would bring the 50-day EMA at $48.49 into focus. The S3 pivot at $46.20 and the lower pitchfork median line near $45.06 sit deeper but maintain the broader uptrend.

This strong structural base coincides with a macro backdrop that remains supportive. Expectations of lower U.S. yields reduce the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets. The possibility of Kevin Hassett assuming a leadership role at the Federal Reserve has added to expectations of deeper policy easing. The physical market remains tight, with refiners and industrial users continuing to absorb dips. Geopolitical tension and weaker global growth projections add further support.

Momentum remains constructive. Price continues to ride the upper channel while RSI trends near mid-range, leaving room for expansion. Bollinger Bands show a tightening formation that typically precedes a strong directional breakout.

In earlier discussions, we noted that silver was entering a tightening consolidation beneath major resistance while maintaining a strong higher-low structure. That view remains intact. The $54 barrier is still the key ceiling to clear before confirming the next leg toward $57 and $60, with dips into the $50-48 zone likely to attract strong buying interest.
