data.title: LightPath Technologies: A Lot Of Promise, But The Sector Seems Overpriced
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to take a look at LightPath’s (LPTH) meteoric share price increase over the last year to see if it was warranted and still a good time to get in. Operations are improving, and the broader outlook seems to be promising; however, the company’s valuation seems to be stretched right now, making me think the growth is already priced in.

The Company

LightPath Technologies is a vertically integrated provider of advanced optics and imaging systems, with a primary focus on defense and commercial markets. The company designs and manufactures custom optical components, assemblies, and infrared cameras and provides engineering services. The components are used in products like thermal imaging and sensing applications for defense, industrial, and commercial sectors. Integrated lens assemblies and camera modules that combine optics with sensors and mechanics, which are highly sought after in the most modern applications, like drones, industrial inspection and automation, and advanced driver-assistance systems.

Briefly on Financials

The company’s sales completely transformed around the end of 2024. 2025 has been a fantastic year for the company as its sales got a second wind.

We can see that two of the company’s four segments experienced significant growth over the year, specifically the Infrared components and Assemblies, and modules. In the most recent earnings report, we can see that these two saw a growth of 63% and a whopping 435%, respectively, helping the company reach an overall y/y growth of around 80% for the first quarter. The massive jump in both of the segments mentioned is not due to organic growth, however, but more due to the company’s acquisition of G5 Infrared, so it is not as good as it looks, but it represents a massive boost to the company’s operations.

Of course, such explosive growth doesn’t come cheap. The company’s margins across the board have taken a hit over the last two years or so, with operating margins and net margins being considerably in the red for a while now. On the plus side, we can see that EBIT margins have somewhat improved from the recent lows but are still quite in the red.

Looking at the latest earnings report, gross margins declined by 400 bps due to “certain nonrecurring or end-of-life orders in the prior year that had higher margins.”

Operating expenses grew 66%, with the increase due to the integration expenses of G5’s acquisition. The management pointed to an improvement on a non-GAAP basis by singling out adjusted EBITDA turning positive at around $360K.

On some other important metrics, the company’s backlog grew to around $90m, representing around three years’ revenues. The backlog gives us visibility and certainty, and we like certainty. Backlog grew around 5% y/y, which isn’t particularly exciting but is growth nonetheless.

Let’s have a look at the company’s financial position. LightPath finished the quarter with around $11.5m in cash and equivalents against around $4.8m in long-term debt. Is this worrisome, considering the company is not profitable? I hardly think so. The company’s cash pile can easily cover the debt outstanding. It is important to note that the company’s cash position has improved considerably since June 2025 due to the recent $8m private equity placement. The management has acknowledged the continued negative GAAP performance but also stressed that the cash burn is decreasing and the company has sufficient liquidity to fund ongoing operations, working capital needs, and strategic initiatives.

This view is supported by the company’s ability to turn positive on adjusted EBITDA, as well as an increase in cash from operating activities to -$1.1m from -$1.7m. So, I think the company will be operating for much longer, and I don’t see it going out of business anytime soon. I’m sure further equity financing may be required, which will dilute shareholders, but if the company’s share price continues to skyrocket, that is not going to be an issue.

Overall, the company’s recent M&A action worked out well in terms of boosting its top line growth considerably; however, it remains to be seen if this is going to be sustained going forward. I believe that once the acquisition makes a full year, we are not going to see such explosive growth, unless there is decent organic growth in the future, in terms of an increase in backlog and new orders. The backlog growing by around 5% is what I would expect to see in the future in the company’s top-line growth.

Comments on the Outlook

Let’s focus on the two mentioned business sectors and why I think they will continue to provide decent growth going forward.

Defense and Aerospace- the key growth sectors

With the recent successful acquisition of G5, the company will continue to see a lot of growth here due to the Defense and Aerospace sectors being very hot right now. These sectors are the largest and fastest-growing sectors for the company, which represent around 70% of total revenues. Key applications here include thermal imaging systems, weapon sights, surveillance, and space and border security.

The global aerospace and defense fiber optic cables market alone looks very promising with a CAGR of 8%-10% through 2034, driven by the adoption of durable fiber optics in defense aircraft and space programs.

Furthermore, governments across the globe are increasing defense spending, which means there should be a lot more money around for companies like LPTH. Due to the conflicts all around the globe, like in Ukraine and Israel, many governments are looking to expand their defense programs with advanced autonomous technology, like UAVs, surveillance satellites, and drones.

Drone operations have been growing a lot and have shown a lot of success in Ukraine, so it is a very promising growth segment for LTPH. Conflict is, of course, not what anyone wants to see, and I would prefer a company not profiting from such a disaster, but it is unavoidable. It is a double-edged sword for the company. If global tensions escalate, the company will most likely see a decent uptick in sales, but that also comes with a supply chain disruption. The recent geopolitical conflicts put some stress on the company’s ability to procure germanium, as China has a dominant global position in the production and processing. China implemented export controls and eventually banned germanium exports to the US due to security concerns. Germanium is a critical component in infrared optics, like thermal imaging and night vision. So, this shortage raised prices and eroded many companies’ profitability.

How is the company looking to bypass this issue? It aims to significantly reduce dependence on germanium via solid innovation like its proprietary BlackDiamond glass. This is a specialized chalcogenide glass developed to be a more abundant, cost-effective, and domestic alternative to germanium, which offers similar or even better performance and mitigates the past supply chain issues. The company is actively transitioning from germanium, but it has still not fully done so, so germanium remains an important material.

The company recently signed contracts with Ondas Holdings (ONDS) and Unusual Machines (UMAC), both of which are leading providers of autonomous robotics and NDAA-compliant drone components, which will accelerate LPTH’s deployment of its advanced optics and thermal imaging in drones that are used for defense, government, and commercial applications.

Valuation

With such an unprofitable situation right now, doing a DCF analysis is not a good way to find what the company is worth, so I decided to do a comps analysis instead. The company, according to its 10-K report, faces competition from start to finish of the product and mentions MKS Instruments’ (MKSI) subsidiary Ophir Optronics Solutions, Teledyne FLIR (TDY), RTX Corp (RTX), and Elbit Systems (ESLT), among others that are not currently trading on the stock exchanges. It’s kind of a limited list, so I’m adding a few extra to my comparison model, but just so you are aware, they may not be perfect substitutes. Below is the list settled on.

We can see that most of the companies are trading at quite high multiples across the board, which isn’t a good start for the company right now. Additionally, the valuation won’t be very accurate since some companies do not have positive earnings and EBITDA like our target company, so according to valuation by multiples, we only get to see comparisons through P/S ratio, P/B ratio, and EV/Revenue.

Since LPTH demands the highest multiples out of the ones available, the valuation tells us that the company is quite overvalued. Taking these multiples into account, we get the company’s intrinsic value below, telling us LPTH is quite overvalued.

This can change if and when the company begins to make a profit on the bottom line, but for now, the separate multiples are clearly telling us LPTH is overvalued.

Closing Comments

Am I looking to invest? It does look quite promising, given the ever-increasing need for AI and autonomous machines in conflicts; however, I would like to see what the growth looks like once the inorganic growth phase passes over the next two quarters. I expect the growth to slow down considerably, and I’m not sure I can see the company becoming profitable any time soon, unless operations improve drastically, which they haven’t as of the latest quarter. The company’s share price skyrocketed this past year, which already makes it a bit less attractive. To me, looks like the aggressive growth was already priced in.

Nevertheless, I am putting the company on my watchlist and will revisit whenever I get more information on its growth potential.

data.title: The Charles Schwab Corporation: Rate Cuts Are Not A Problem (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of The Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 11%. Given the importance of cash economics to its business, shares have softened as the Fed has begun its rate cutting cycle. However, I actually see the company as positioned fairly well for a moderate cutting cycle. I last covered Schwab in August, rating the stock a “hold,” and since then, shares have lost about 5% while the market has added 6%, suggesting even a “sell” rating was merited. With shares seemingly range bound and further potential Fed rate cuts on the horizon, now is a good time to revisit SCHW. I am more bullish.

Forge Purchase Makes Strategic Sense

In early November, Schwab announced a deal to buy Forge Global (FRGE) for $660 million. While a relatively small deal, SCHW paid a large premium of 72% (I view premiums above 40% as being unusually high). This suggests there were likely several interested parties. Plus the premium basically amounts to ~$0.25 per SCHW share, so even if it overpaid a bit, the overpayment is not particularly material to SCHW relative to the strategic purpose of the acquisition.

Namely, Forge operates a marketplace of private investments, an area where Schwab is lacking exposure. By integrating Forge into its Alternative Investments platform, Schwab will make it easier for its advisors and clients to invest in private securities, an increasing focus especially with eased regulatory rules for these securities in 401(K) retirement plans.

Forge has 3 million users and 625 private companies listed on its platform, with about $17 billion of transactions. Frankly, Forge is not going to be a material direct contributor to SCHW’s results. Rather, the purpose of this acquisition is to immediately scale a private securities platform rather than building one from scratch with SCHW taking this platform and gradually rolling it across its customer base, including launching a fund to add AUM. With growing demand for private securities, this acquisition ensures that Schwab maintains a leading-edge product offering to continue accumulating client assets.

Asset Gathering Has Been Strong

Right now, the company is excelling on that front. In mid-November, Schwab provided a monthly update on assets. Net new assets were an excellent $138 billion, up 44% from last year, as the company continues to win market share. That brings client assets to $11.8 trillion, up 20% from last year thanks to both fund flows and market appreciation. This strong NNA performance continues a trend we have seen, with Q3 coming in 44% above last year’s level at $138 billion.

In October, highly lucrative margin loans were up 7% sequentially, and with sizable market volatility, trading remains elevated at 8.6 million daily trades. I was encouraged to see cash sweep balances rise by $3.2 billion to $429 billion, primarily due to typical seasonal trends. Cash utilization was down to 9.3% from 9.8% a year ago. With rates relatively elevated, we have seen customers carry less cash to invest in higher yielding money market funds and short-dated fixed income. This had been a source of pressure on Schwab’s earnings, but with much of this played out, earnings growth has been excellent.

In the company’s third quarter, Charles Schwab earned $1.31, which was up 70% from last year, as revenue grew 27% to $6.1 billion. Earnings were also $0.06 ahead of consensus. Operating margins continue to be best-in-class at 51.3%. This was up by 10.1% last year as net interest recovered, speaking to the tremendous operating leverage in the business. While Schwab grew trading and advisory fees, net interest revenue was the standout at $3.1 billion, and this remains the core driver of the company’s profitability.

Net Interest Trends Are Favorable

In Q3, Schwab paid down more expensive wholesale funding by $13 billion, which should continue to support net interest margins. Schwab, like most cash sweep custodians, began to see balance pressure intensify after the failure of Silicon Valley Bank in early 2023, as that failure raised the issue of how much cash was sitting in low-yielding accounts. As Schwab saw clients reduce their cash positions, it was forced to rely on more expensive wholesale funding, reaching a peak of nearly $100 billion. Over time, as its business has grown, Schwab has been able to work down this expensive funding, bringing wholesale funding back to the upper-end of its $5-$15 billion target.

In Q3, Schwab’s NIM expanded 78bps to 2.86%. Its funding costs fell to 85bps from 1.61%, primarily due to lower wholesale funding as well as pass-through of the Fed rate cuts. This will likely migrate a bit lower with Fed rate cuts, but just by ~20-30% as much as Fed rate cuts vs the ~75% beta so far.

On the other side of its balance sheet, asset yields actually improved 2bps to 3.71%, as increased securities lending revenue and favorable mix shift offset lower yields on floating rate securities. Like many banks. Schwab is saddled with fixed-rate securities bought when rates were lower. It has ~$207 billion of securities at a ~1.8% yield. As these mature, SCHW can reinvest them at higher market yields. With a ~4 year duration on these securities, this will be a prolonged process.

Assuming ~3-4 Fed rate cuts over the next year, I expect the benefits of fixed reinvestment to offset the negatives on floating-rate assets and leave its portfolio’s yield roughly flat. If the Fed cuts more than 4 times, we likely would see the portfolio yield fall somewhat, and with fewer cuts, we would see the portfolio yield rise. With funding costs likely to fall somewhat further, this means that SCHW’s NIM should continue to expand and likely surpass 3% by mid-2026, which will help support incremental earnings.

Shares Are Now A Buy

Additionally, Schwab maintains a 9.7% regulatory tier 1 leverage ratio, flat to last year and well above regulatory minimums, providing ample capacity for buybacks, dividends, and bolt-on M&A like the Forge deal. Given its excellent capital position, Schwab bought back $2.7 billion of stock in the third quarter. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 1.3% from last year. Shares also pay a 1.2% dividend yield.

Back in August, I was targeting about $4.60-$5.00 in earnings over the next year. Since then, the market has moved a bit higher, supporting increased fee-based revenue. With the passage of time, we also continue to move into higher earning periods as NIM should steadily expand at least through the next 12 months, and Schwab’s balance sheet reshaping has progressed a bit more quickly than I hoped. In calendar 2026, I am looking for the company to earn $5.35-$5.70, depending on cash balance trends and assuming the S&P 500 remains around current levels. This assumes no material impact from Forge. While I believe the deal makes clear strategic sense, I do not expect a material impact on results for 18+ months.

Shares are now below 16.5x as its multiple has compressed notably. That comes even as I expect SCHW’s NIM to expand modestly over the next year, leaving SCHW relatively well-positioned for Fed rate cuts. Plus, lower rates may also reduce pressure on cash balances, further supporting earning. For a company with organic growth or 4-5% plus the benefits of market appreciation, I view this as an attractive multiple, as those factors support combined earnings growth of 8-10%. As such, I am upgrading shares to a “buy” and see shares passing $100 or 18x earnings, reflecting its growing core business and favorable NIM trends.

data.title: Waste Management: Stericycle Acquisition Is A Winner But Not Enough At This Valuation
data.description:
Q3 2025 Earnings Takeaway

For Q3 2025, Waste Management, Inc.(WM) delivered a revenue of $6.44 billion and an EBITDA of $1.718 billion, up by around 15% and 2.44% YoY, respectively.

Adjusting for one-time expenses from Stericycle integration costs and impairment charge from the temporary operation shutdown in one of WM's recycling businesses, adjusted EBITDA is $1.970 billion, up by 15% YoY. Free cash flow generation for the quarter is $821 million, up from $618 million last year.

Turning over to segmental performance, which is WM labeled as Legacy Businesses, consisting of its Collection and Disposal, Energy, and Recycling, and WM Healthcare Solutions, formerly known as Stericycle.

WM Legacy Business revenue is $5.815 billion, up 3.7% YoY. WM Legacy growth is lower than last year's due to the drag from WM's recycling business. According to the management, recycled commodity prices declined by almost 35%. The introduction of automation and technology managed to offset this commodity decline, which led to the recycling business's EBITDA growing by 18%.

Last year's acquisition, Stericycle, now WM Healthcare Solutions, delivered a revenue of $628 million with an EBITDA of $89 million. Excluding integration costs such as severance, retention costs, and ERP implementation, WM Healthcare's adjusted EBITDA is around $110 million, with an EBITDA margin of 17.5%.

This is a quarterly good result for WM. Despite the maturity of the core legacy business and the cyclical headwind of its commodity segment, the company continues to find ways to expand profitability through introduction of automation and technology to improve the company's efficiency and productivity. WM's core disposal and collection business is very stable, considering the significant barriers to entry in this business. An investor could extrapolate from its historical growth rate with certainty. Most of WM's market value comes from this business.

However, what I'm interested in is the latest significant strategic move that WM has made, which is the Stericycle acquisition. This business segment will be a key growth area for WM and, as will be detailed below, will likely deliver value creation for shareholders.

Stericycle Historical Brief Takeaway

On November 4, 2025, WM completed its acquisition of Stericycle for an enterprise value of $7.2 billion. At first, when assessing the acquisition, I'm skeptical about the acquisition. Before the acquisition, Stericycle had been on a clear financial decline for multiple years, which appears to be secular in nature.

The decline has been attributed to the consolidation of hospitals, which increases their bargaining power due to increased size and scale. This customer consolidation has negatively affected Stericycle's pricing power. Additionally, Stericycle has undertaken poor M&A decisions in the past, resulting in excessive debt and poor integration outcomes, which exacerbated the problem.

Stericycle uses specialized equipment and assets to collect medical waste, which is more complex to collect than traditional waste. This specialized equipment and procedure for collecting medical waste entail a higher cost than the traditional waste that WM has catered to. However, after assessing the cost synergies the combined enterprise could realize and the potential of applying WM operational know-how into Stericycle, I believe that this is a very good acquisition for WM.

Cost Synergy Alone Makes The Stericycle Acquisition Value Accretive To Shareholders

But first, let's analyze the numbers involved in the Stericycle acquisition.

Using Stericycle's 2024 TTM EBITDA of around $400 million and an acquisition enterprise value of $7.2 billion, this translates to an acquisition EV/EBITDA multiple of around 18x, above WM's EV/EBITDA multiple around the acquisition. In any M&A, an acquirer needs to pay a premium to entice the acquiree's shareholders to sell. However, the need to pay a price premium is the usual bane of any M&A transaction, as the premium paid is often excessive relative to the potential synergy value that could be realized.

To calculate the potential value creation of an M&A transaction, I use the following formula: M&A Value Creation = Value Of The Synergy - Premium Paid.

Remember this formula, as we will get back to this later. Now, how much premium did WM pay for the Stericycle acquisition? To calculate the premium, we multiply Stericycle's 2024 EBITDA of around $400 million by WM's EV/EBITDA multiple around the acquisition, which is around 15x. This will translate to around $6 billion enterprise value for Stericycle. At an acquisition enterprise value of $7.2 billion, the premium is around $1.2 billion.

This constitutes a premium of around 20%. Coincidentally, 20% is also the premium that WM paid for Stericycle's shareholders. But what's the rationale for using this kind of method?

Often, the stock market's judgment of a particular stock is reflective of its real value. Using the market leader's valuation multiple, in this case WM, is often a representative of that industry's fundamentals and potential.

Following the completion of Stericycle's acquisition, the management projected a run-rate cost synergy exceeding $125 million, with $80 million to $100 million expected to be achieved by the end of 2025. The company, in its investor day, would later double this run-rate cost synergy from $125 million to $250 million by 2027.

The initial run-rate cost synergy alone pretty much justifies this deal. One method to calculate if the premium was justified is to divide the run-rate cost synergy by the combined enterprise's cost of capital or discount rate. The purpose of the calculation is to determine the value to perpetuity of the synergy. For strong companies like WM, I simply subtract the minimum return that investors should demand for an equity investment, usually around 8% to 10%, by the most likely future US GDP growth of around 2% to 3%. Then I compare it to the risk-free rate and subjectively determine the appropriate discount rate based on my qualitative assessment. Determining the appropriate discount rate is more of an art than a science. I always demand a discount rate higher than a risk-free rate, as no matter how strong the company's fundamentals, there will always be extra risk associated with an equity investment. I believe WM's cost of capital is around 7% to 8%. Now it is at the investor's whim to arbitrarily settle for a lower cost of capital of, say, 6% to 7%, after his own diligence, but I prefer to be conservative, so I will settle for a higher one.

By dividing the run-rate cost savings of around $125 million by 8%, the value to perpetuity of the cost synergy should be around $1.56 billion. Clearly, there is an excess of $360 million. This excess value is the value creation of the acquisition.

Moreover, suppose the management achieved the revised run-rate cost savings it foresees by 2027 of around $250 million, the acquisition value creation will be around $1.925 billion.

Can an investor assume that this cost synergy is attainable? After all, these are all future assumptions, which are always uncertain, especially in the heavily regulated business of WM.

I believe it is. The justification for a premium price paid in an acquisition comes from two sources: cost and revenue synergy. Ideally, an investor should prefer an acquisition premium that is justified mostly by cost synergy, or even better solely by it.

Reducing costs is always a more controllable endeavor than realizing revenue synergy, which often involves cross-selling that relies on unpredictable demand. A simple question to ask when assessing the revenue synergy presented by a management in an acquisition is, 'Will customers value the products sold by the combined enterprise?' I definitely see a cross-selling opportunity for WM, like offering solid waste management service to Stericycle's existing customers. However, I still prefer that the justification for a premium is mainly from cost synergy.

Aside from the generic removal of redundant SG&A function, cost synergy can be realized from the increased scale economics of logistics functions such as fleet repairs and maintenance, and Stericycle gaining access to WM's operational know-how.

According to WM's Head of Investor Relations Ed Egl, a significant portion of the $250 million cost synergy WM has forecasted will come from SG&A.

And then on top of that, we double our synergies from 125 to 250, right? A lot of that's going to be coming from SG&A, and that's going to bring their SG&A roughly to about 15%, 16% of revenue. As you know, today, we're running around 9%, right? So in three years from now, there's probably more opportunity to get that SG&A a little bit lower. None of these synergies contemplate cross-selling opportunities as well, right? There's an additional revenue opportunities here.

WM management's progress on this matter has been significant. And it is evident in the chart below, where the SG&A intensity(SG&A / Revenue) of Stericycle shows a substantial decline since the acquisition.

Clearly, with the combination, Stericycle has been able to leverage WM's corporate overhead function. And more importantly, Stericycle has gained access to the more intangible capability of WM, such as its culture, process, and best practices. WM management sees this declining to 17% in the future and judging the trend, I believe it is reasonable to assume that this target is achievable.

Lastly, Stericycle will benefit from WM's access to cheap cost of capital due to strong cash generation and access to cheap debt.

The Importance Of An Optimal Capital Structure For WM

I believe one of the most important factors that an investor needs to assess when investing in a mature but stable company, such as WM, is an optimal capital structure. When a business is stable and has strong profitability or cash flow generation, it should carry more debt, and if it's cyclical and rapidly changing, then more equity with little to no debt. The ultimate objective of an optimal capital structure is to maximize the productivity of the company's equity without disregarding the requirements of the business. Additionally, an optimal capital structure should lessen WM's cost of capital due to efficient use of debt and equity as debt is often cheaper than equity, which is crucial to WM's future acquisitions. The acquiree would be able to leverage WM's cheap cost of capital to fund its expenditures.

When considering the inherent stability and cash flow generation of WM, it has the capability to have a more aggressive capital structure.

Additionally, an optimal capital structure is often indicative of the quality of the management's capital allocation. A company with sound capital allocation returns excess capital when it makes sense to do so. As mentioned earlier, an optimal capital structure allows a more productive use of equity capital, especially among mature and profitable companies, due to efficient use of leverage. This is evident in the chart below. Despite earning a return on capital employed of around 10%, which, I believe, is average, the company was able to earn an impressive return on equity.

WM is one of those unique cases that, despite the seemingly capped ability of the business to generate a high return on capital employed, the inherent stability of the business model enables the company to use more debt to make its equity more productive. This is common among regulated industries. The force of regulation limits a company's ability to generate a high return on its capital employed. However, the very same forces of regulation itself may provide the stability to the business through increased barriers to entry that enable adoption of more debt in the balance sheet. For WM, the complex regulatory environment, heavy capital intensity, and the high minimum efficient scale provide significant barriers to entry that give stability, as proven by its long periods of financial and operational stability.

Valuation

WM is a company that can be categorized as a mature and low-growth company. Ideally, you want these types of mature companies to possess the following attributes: a high free cash flow conversion rate, consistently high returns on equity capital, efficient use of leverage, and a consistent dividend payer or frequently buy back their shares. In my opinion, this kind of company attracts value-oriented investors rather than growth ones. And WM fits all these attributes. However, another crucial factor is the price an investor should pay, as paying a growth-like valuation for a mature company can hinder decent returns for an investor. Historically, WM's revenue and EBITDA have grown at a CAGR of around 6.2% and 7.6%, respectively.

While respectable growth for a mature company, it doesn't justify WM's P/E multiple of around 34x, which is slightly higher than the S&P 500's current P/E of around 30x. This P/E ratio translates to around 3.12% earnings yield, which is a premium when compared to the long-term risk-free rate of around 4.7%(US 30Y bond yield) and doesn't compensate enough for the extra risk that is always present in equities. Additionally, relative to its historical EV/EBIT multiple, WM is currently selling on the upper end of its historical multiple.

Now, one may argue that good companies should sell at a higher valuation as it reflects the quality of the company. Quality companies are those that possess stability, high returns on capital, and cash generation, which I believe WM possesses. After all, the judgment of the market often reflects the true value of a particular stock. I sometimes agree with this statement. However, an investor needs to always consider the price he is paying. Paying a high price for a quality company exposes the investor to the risk of underperformance due to the embedded high expectation from the market, which makes it harder and harder for the company to outperform this expectation. An investor shouldn't think that the market's judgment is wrong, but only that he disagrees with it. It's not about being right or wrong about the future, but rather an investor positioning himself in scenarios where the odds of generating an adequate return are in his favor. Paying for a growth-like valuation for a mature company doesn't put an investor in a position where the odds are in his favor. Therefore, I will call a 'Hold' rating for this stock.
